Home Essays Transcripts Articles About
NE

Do You See What Is Present

April 27, 2026 ·

(Hillside Hermitage Talk no.48hh - edited transcript)

Majjhima Nikāya 38

Viññāṇa:

Prefix: vi- (apart, distinction, separation)
Root: √jñā (Sanskrit) / √ñā (Pali) (to know, to cognize)
Suffix: -ana (verbal noun of action)
Literal Meaning: “Knowing apart” or “discriminative cognition.”
Common translation: Consciousness.

MN38 Mahātaṇhāsaṅkhaya sutta

The Longer Discourse on the Ending of Craving:
Now at that time a bhikkhu called Sāti, the fisherman’s son, had the following pernicious view: “As I understand the Auspicious One’s teaching, it is this very same consciousness that roams and transmigrates, not another.

The Nature of Consciousness

Ajahn Nyanamoli: Even before we go further, people might too easily dismiss that: “Of course, that’s a wrong view.” But you want to ask yourself: do you really know why it is wrong? Not just because the Buddha said so, but do you see for yourself why he said it is a wrong view?

That would already require somebody to start clarifying the designation consciousness or viññāṇa, whatever you want to call it. People might realize that very often they’ve been using these words without any clarity as to what they actually mean. What are you designating when you say consciousness? To yourself, not to others. Just sit with yourself and say, consciousness, consciousness. What is consciousness? What are you referring to?

That’s how you begin this process of clarification or vipassana.

The reason why that view is wrong, is because any notion of “this is what gets reborn,” or simply asking “what gets reborn?“—that already assumes that something gets reborn. Any of those attitudes requires an assumption of an external point of view to your experience right here, right now. And that in itself is attavāda.

Because he has a wrong view in regard to his own experience, from that he then assumed further that it is this consciousness that moves around and goes to the next life. Because of that attavāda, the assumption of the external self that he’s not just failing to undo, he’s clearly adding more to it with all these other views he’s developing. Because of all of that, he’s actually harming himself.

Your only reference point is the experience you have. This life, that’s it. So when you think about before or after this life, it’s within this experience as a whole. Your assuming there is this life and then the next life is all within this experience, right here, right now. That whole thing cannot be proven; you cannot see it the way you think you’re seeing it, which is from an external manner where you can step aside and see yourself here and say, “Yes, when this bit goes, this is the other part that moves to the next life.” Nobody can see things like that, not even the Buddha himself. So assuming such a view is entirely mistaken from the point of view of the nature of things.

Bhikkhu: What if someone says that it’s the same consciousness that is in every experience that I experience? So yesterday’s is the same consciousness as it is today.

Nm: Again, that statement would be rooted in the assumption of the external point of view in regard to consciousness. Or, like some interpretations say, there are “moments of consciousness” that last a certain number of milliseconds. That’s inconceivable. Not just that you can’t measure other people’s consciousness, but you can’t even see your own.

When people say, “No, in your samādhi, time stands still and then you can see,” you can’t. As the Buddha described, what is present in samādhi is nāma-rūpa-viññāṇa; it’s the five aggregates that are present in samādhi. So whether it’s samādhi or not, your experience is there as a whole. You thinking that samādhi gives you some special power of stopping time and stepping outside of the five aggregates to witness it is just a fantasy.

The Buddha said that in samādhi, there is still contact, there is still attention, there is still feeling, there is still mindfulness. He also said even when people remember previous lives, they remember one of the aggregates right here, right now. You can’t step outside of this structure.

In the same sense, your yesterdays and your tomorrows are within your today. Your previous life and your future life are also within your today. So you projecting this linear view of cosmology, of your rebirth and what moves, is completely gratuitous and utterly divorced from any kind of reflexive recognition and discernment.

Consciousness as “Presence of Things”

Consciousness literally means presence of things. You can’t even think about pure presence; that’s utterly meaningless, and as mystical as it sounds. What is presence? You discern presence on account of things that are present, on account of phenomena that have arisen. That’s the conscious aspect of the arisen experience.

So you, mystifying that presence as some sort of energy—well, it’s clearly a thought in your mind, which means that thought is present. This means that thought cannot be that presence which consciousness is. That’s just how it works. The fact that you imagined it means you are conscious of it. You can’t be conscious of your consciousness.

Bhikkhu: There’s that saying that wisdom is to be developed, consciousness is to be understood.

Nm: Yes, understood. Not seen directly, because that would require you to step outside of this situation as a whole. You can discern it, you can understand it. And by doing so, you develop wisdom. By understanding consciousness, you understand the nature of presence, the nature of arising, the nature of manifestation. Bhikkhu: And where you are. Nm: Well, there won’t be much left of you if you understand things correctly in that sense. You maintain your sense of self through that external assumption to your own experience. That’s just how it works: attavāda. It’s as subtle as that. It’s not like a puthujjana (an ordinary person) goes around saying, “Oh, I think I am this or I am that.” No, it’s just a subtle assumption that you can see yourself in an external manner, for example, or that you can see the next day in a manner that’s independent of you. An assumption of a public world, a public space and time that we are all in. All those things go back to that subtle factor of attavāda—the assumption of the external point of view, one way or the other. Bhikkhu: The subtle assumption of an external point of view in regard to experience as a whole. Nm: In regard to your own self. That is why his view is pernicious.

The Bhikkhus Confront Bhikkhu Sāti

Several bhikkhus heard about this. They approached Sāti and said to him, “Is it really true, Venerable Sāti, that you have such a pernicious view: ‘As I understand the Auspicious One’s teaching, it is this very same consciousness that roams and transmigrates, not another’?” “Absolutely, venerables. As I understand the Auspicious One’s teaching, it is this very same consciousness that roams and transmigrates, not another.” Then, wishing to dissuade Sāti from his view, the bhikkhus pursued, pressed, and grilled him, “Do not say that, Sāti! Do not misrepresent the Auspicious One, for misrepresentation of the Auspicious One is not good. And the

5

Auspicious One would not say that. In many ways the Auspicious One has said that consciousness is dependently arisen, since without support, consciousness cannot exist.”

Nm: As I said, there is no presence without something being present. As the Buddha said, consciousness and the things you are conscious of (nāma-rūpa) are like two sticks simultaneously propping each other up. You remove one, the other cannot remain standing. And that’s not a momentary thing you can observe, because if you think you’re observing it, it means you’re assuming you’re outside of it. You can’t have presence unless there is something present. Even when you think the word “presence” in your mind, that is present for you. Thoughts are present, imagination, abstraction—it is present, which means you are conscious of it. That’s how you can know things at any given time: because they’re present. But people detach “presence” from the things it is the presence of. And then when they think about “the pure presence,” “the presence of awareness”—they fail to see that that idea itself is an arisen thing that is present for them, and thus cannot stand for that presence of that very thing. And he, Sāti, clearly assumed that that presence is independent and it moves around. So they said to him, didn’t the Buddha say that consciousness cannot stand on its own? It’s not a question of whether it’s the same consciousness or a different one. The point is consciousness of any kind is inconceivable without something to be conscious of simultaneously. And you can’t step outside of it to see it, because you are second to it. Bhikkhu Sāti, clearly did not see that view as a harmful view. From his point of view, he thought that’s what the Buddha was teaching. He thought the other monks were wrong.

The Buddha Questions Bhikkhu Sāti

So the Auspicious One addressed one of the bhikkhus, “Please, bhikkhu, in my name tell the bhikkhu Sāti that the teacher summons him.”

6

“Yes, venerable sir,” that bhikkhu replied. He went to Sāti and said to him, “Venerable Sāti, the teacher summons you.” “Yes, venerable,” Venerable Sāti replied. He went to the Auspicious One, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Auspicious One said to him, “Is it really true, Sāti, that you have such a pernicious view: ‘As I understand the Auspicious One’s teaching, it is this very same consciousness that roams and transmigrates, not another’?” “Absolutely, venerable sir. As I understand the Auspicious One’s teaching, it is this very same consciousness that roams and transmigrates, not another.” “Sāti, what is that consciousness?” “Venerable sir, he is the speaker, the knower who experiences the results of good and bad actions in all the different realms.” “Misguided man, who in the world have you ever known me to teach in that way? Have not I said in many ways that consciousness is dependently arisen, since without a support, consciousness cannot exist? But still you misrepresent me by your wrong grasp, harm yourself, and create much wickedness. This will be for your lasting harm and suffering.” Then the Auspicious One said to the bhikkhus, “What do you think, bhikkhus? Is this bhikkhu Sāti even warmed up in this teaching-and-discipline?” “How could that be, venerable sir? No, venerable sir.” When this was said, Sāti sat silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say. Knowing this, the Auspicious One said, “Misguided man, you will be known by your own pernicious view. I will question the bhikkhus about this.”

Nm: That’s a contemporary view of consciousness that many people have. Most people think it’s your consciousness that does all this, that gets the retribution of kamma, that gets reborn. The fact that you even refer to it as “that” means you have

7

assumed it, as if you can see it as an object: “Oh, it’s that which does all these things.” You fail to see that the “that” you’re designating is a present thing, which means there is a presence in regard to that thing. That’s what consciousness is—that presence, not what you’re designating it to be. The Buddha publicly, in front of the whole assembly, called him a fool because of that view. Not because he’d done anything wrong by body, speech, or mind. He just misunderstood what the Buddha was teaching. And that’s how serious it is. The Buddha says you’ve not just injured yourself but stored up much future demerit, because he’s been going around misrepresenting the Buddha. That’s all it takes. He’s responsible for it because he wouldn’t have arrived at it without willfully ignoring the fact that he already knows he hasn’t fully penetrated and understood the Dhamma. He basically, through his carelessness, ignored that. That would have made him concerned before he started speaking about what the Buddha is teaching. The fact that he was going around saying, “This is what the Lord teaches,” coming out of his own lack of effort to understand—that’s why it’s so bad. And now two and a half millennia later, his name is still associated with it.

The Nature of Presence and Nutriment “Whatever condition consciousness arises dependent upon, it is classified by that very condition. Consciousness that arises dependent upon the eye and forms is classified as eye-consciousness. Consciousness that arises dependent upon the ear and sounds is classified as ear-consciousness; dependent upon the nose and odors, as nose-consciousness; dependent upon the tongue and tastes, as tongue-consciousness; dependent upon the physical body and tangibles, as physical body-consciousness; and dependent upon the mano and dhammā, as mano-consciousness.

”

Nm: If sight is present, it’s the presence of sights. Likewise with sounds, smell, tastes, touches. The presence of these things is what consciousness is. Reasoning about consciousness as a separate entity is completely mistaken.

8

“Just as a fire is reckoned by the particular condition dependent on which it burns. When fire burns dependent on logs, it is reckoned as a log fire. If it burns dependent on sticks, it is reckoned as a stick fire.”

Nm: You can discern that it doesn’t matter what is present; what matters is that there is a presence there in itself, of any kind. So whether it’s sight, sound, touches, thoughts—doesn’t matter, a presence is there. That’s if you try to understand it from the point of view of the aggregates. When you stop being concerned with even that relatively non-particular designation of “presence of sights,” which is quite a general designation… you can go even more broadly, more peripherally, and that’s how you start discerning the aggregates. You say, “It doesn’t matter what sense the consciousness is the presence of. Consciousness is there, presence is there.” And that’s why consciousness is the last aggregate you can discern. “Bhikkhus, do you see: ‘This is’?” “Yes.” “Bhikkhus, do you see: ‘Its origination occurs with that as nutriment’?”

Nm: For example, take a sight. We look at these mountains and say, “Do you see that these mountains are present?” Yes. Now, would you be able to see them if there’s nothing there to be present? If there’s no eye to see, if there are no forms to be seen? If any of those things, which are utterly out of your control, were not there, would you be able to say, “This is present?” You wouldn’t. It’s all rooted in that thing that’s beneath you, underneath you. You can’t even conceive it. Without anything to be present, can you talk about presence? So they say, of course not. There needs to be a necessary condition for it, a nutriment. “Do you see: ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is subject to cessation’?”

9

Nm: Yes. While you’re looking at these mountains, imagine your optical cord gets severed and you go blind. Would you still be able to have the experience of that sight? You wouldn’t. Yet, would you be able to stop that nutriment from disappearing? No. That’s the definition of nutriment. They’re not in your control. They’re independent of you. Yes, nutriment is something you eat, but you can only do so because it made itself available. In other words, it’s not rooted in you. “Bhikkhus, does doubt arise when one is uncertain thus: ‘Has this come to be?’”

Nm: Whichever aspect you’re not sure about, that’s where doubt arises. And that’s how you go beyond doubt: upon the most fundamental principle of paṭiccasamuppāda. With this, this is. Or with this present, this is present. The Raft Simile and Letting Go of the View “Bhikkhus, is doubt abandoned in one who sees as it actually is with proper wisdom thus: ‘This has come to be’?” “Yes” “Is doubt abandoned in one who sees as it actually is with proper wisdom: ‘Its presence occurs with that as nutriment’?” Yes. “Is doubt abandoned in one who sees as it actually is with proper wisdom: ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is subject to cessation’?” Yes.

Nm: That’s basically fully clearing any notion of yourself being involved there. You see the arisen experience of sight. Then you see the necessary basis for that experience to arise. Then you see that that basis is utterly out of your control, inconceivable. It’s inconceivable that you could stop your heart from having a heart attack. You fully understand that, and you stop the most gratuitous, the subtlest of

10

assumptions you might have in terms of conceit, vanity, sense of control, self-view. All of that will cease because that becomes inconceivable. “Bhikkhus, has it been seen well by you as it actually is with proper wisdom thus: ‘This has come to be’?” And they all say, “Yes, venerable sir.” “Bhikkhus, purified and bright as this view is, if you adhere to it, cherish it, treasure it, and treat it as a possession, would you then understand that the Dhamma has been taught as similar to a raft, being for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of grasping?” They say, “No, venerable sir.” “Bhikkhus, purified and bright as this view is, if you do not adhere to it, cherish it, and treat it as a possession, would you then understand that the Dhamma has been taught as similar to a raft, being for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of grasping?” “Yes, venerable sir.”

Nm: So they were all at least sotāpannas. They all see the Dhamma, they all have the clear view. But some of them might not be exposing that view, not dwelling on it, because it’s such an amazing view they might already be happy with it. But the Buddha says, don’t just cherish it and put it on a shelf. Use it. Use it and use it, because it will only get stronger, and then you’ll go beyond it. Don’t be lazy, don’t be distracted. “Bhikkhus, remember this discourse of mine briefly as the deliverance in the destruction of craving. But remember the bhikkhu Sāti, son of a fisherman, as caught up in a vast net of craving, in a trammel of craving.”

11

Majjhima Nikāya 38

Mahātaṇhāsaṅkhayasutta: The Greater Discourse on the Destruction of Craving Thus have I heard— At one time the Buddha was staying in Sāvatthī at Jeta’s Grove, in Anāthapiṇḍika’s park. The Pernicious View of Sāti At that time, a pernicious view had arisen in a bhikkhu named Sāti, the fisherman’s son: “As I understand the dhamma taught by the Buddha, it is this very consciousness [cognition] that wanders and transmigrates, and not another.” Several bhikkhus heard about this and approached Sāti, asking him: “Is it true, friend Sāti, that you hold this pernicious view?” “Yes, friends,” Sāti replied. “As I understand the dhamma taught by the Buddha, it is this very consciousness that wanders and transmigrates, and not another.” Desiring to detach Sāti from this pernicious view, the bhikkhus pressed, questioned, and cross-examined him: “Do not speak like this, friend Sāti! Do not misrepresent the Buddha. It is not good to misrepresent the Buddha, for he would not say such a thing. In many ways, the Buddha has stated that consciousness is dependently arisen, and that apart from a condition, there is no origination of consciousness.” Yet, even while being pressed, questioned, and cross-examined, Sāti stubbornly grasped and adhered to his pernicious view, repeating his assertion.

The Buddha Interrogates Sāti

Unable to detach him from this view, the bhikkhus approached the Buddha, paid homage, sat to one side, and reported the entire matter. The Buddha then addressed a certain bhikkhu:
“Come, bhikkhu, address the bhikkhu Sāti in my name, saying: ‘The Teacher calls you, friend Sāti.’”

12

Consenting, the bhikkhu went to Sāti and relayed the message. Sāti went to the Buddha, paid homage, and sat to one side. The Buddha asked him: “Is it true, Sāti, that you hold this pernicious view?” “Yes, Bhante.” “What is this consciousness, Sāti?” “It is that which speaks, feels [experiences affective tone], and reaps the result of good and evil actions here and there, Bhante.” “Misguided person! To whom do you understand me to have taught the dhamma in this way? Have I not stated in many ways that consciousness is dependently arisen, and that apart from a condition, there is no origination of consciousness? Yet you, misguided person, misrepresent us by your own wrong grasp, destroying yourself and generating much demerit. This will lead to your harm and dukkha for a long time.” The Buddha then turned to the other bhikkhus: “What do you think? Has this bhikkhu Sāti kindled even a spark of wisdom in this dhamma and discipline?” “How could he, Bhante? No.” At this, Sāti sat silent, dismayed, his shoulders drooping and his head bowed, pondering without a response. Knowing his state, the Buddha said to him: “You will be recognized by your own pernicious view, misguided person. Now I will question the bhikkhus.”

The Dependent Origination of Consciousness

“Do you understand the dhamma taught by me in the same way this misguided person does?” “No, Bhante. In many ways, you have told us that consciousness is dependently arisen.” “Good. It is good that you understand my teachings this way. Whatever condition consciousness arises dependent upon, it is classified by that very condition. Consciousness that arises dependent upon the eye and forms is classified as eye-consciousness. Consciousness that arises dependent upon the ear and sounds is classified as ear-consciousness; dependent upon the nose and odors, as nose-consciousness; dependent upon the tongue and tastes, as

13

tongue-consciousness; dependent upon the physical body and tangibles, as physical body-consciousness; and dependent upon the mano and dhammā, as mano-consciousness. Just as a fire is classified by whatever condition it burns dependent upon—a fire burning wood is a wood-fire, a fire burning rubbish is a rubbish-fire—so too is consciousness classified by its condition. “Do you see that this has come to be?” “Yes, Bhante.” “Do you see that it originates from that nutriment [sustenance]?” “Yes, Bhante.” “Do you see that when that nutriment ceases, what has come to be is subject to cessation?” “Yes, Bhante.” “If you cling to, cherish, treasure, and treat as your own this view that is so pure and bright, would you be understanding the dhamma that I have taught as similar to a raft, meant for crossing over and not for grasping?” “No, Bhante.”

The Four Nutriments and Dependent Origination

“There are these four nutriments for the maintenance of beings that have come to be, or for the support of those seeking being. What are the four? Edible nutriment, whether coarse or subtle; phassa is the second; mano-volition is the third; and consciousness is the fourth.

What is the source of these four nutriments? They have craving as their source, origin, birth, and provenance. What is the source of craving? Feeling. What is the source of feeling? Phassa. What is the source of phassa? The six sense media. What is the source of the six sense media? Name and form. What is the source of name and form? Consciousness. What is the source of consciousness? Sankhara. What is the source of sankhara? Avijjā.

14

“Thus: With avijjā as condition, sankhara; with sankhara as condition, consciousness; with consciousness as condition, name and form; with name and form as condition, the six sense media; with the six sense media as condition, phassa; with phassa as condition, feeling; with feeling as condition, craving; with craving as condition, upādāna; with upādāna as condition, being; with being as condition, birth; with birth as condition, aging and death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair arise. This is the origination of this entire mass of dukkha. “When you say ‘with birth as condition, aging and death’, is it so?” “Yes, Bhante.” (The Buddha systematically goes through every example of the dependent origination principle forward and backward with them.) “Good. You speak thus, and I also speak thus: When this is, this is; when this arises, this arises. But with the remainderless fading away and cessation of avijjā comes the cessation of sankhara… and so on, until the cessation of this entire mass of dukkha. “Knowing and seeing this, would you run back to the past, wondering: ‘Were we in the past?’ or run to the future, wondering: ‘Will we be in the future?’” “No, Bhante.” “Do you only speak of what you yourselves have known, seen, and understood?” “Yes, Bhante.” “Good. I have brought you to this visible dhamma, which is immediate, inviting inspection, applicable, and to be personally experienced by the wise.

The Process of Conception and Sensory Bondage

“The descent of the embryo takes place through the conjunction of three things. If the mother and father come together, but the mother is not in season and the gandhabba (the being to be born) is not present, there is no descent. Only when all three conjoin does the embryo descend. The mother carries this heavy burden in her womb with great risk for nine or ten months, and after birth, nourishes the child with her own blood, which in the Noble One’s discipline is called mother’s milk. When the boy grows up and his faculties mature, he plays with children’s toys. Eventually, he amuses himself, endowed and supplied with the five strands of kāma—forms cognizable by the eye, sounds cognizable by the ear, odors cognizable by the nose, tastes cognizable by the tongue, and tangibles cognizable by the physical body—all of which are wished for, desired, pleasing, agreeable, connected to kāma, and provoking passion.

“Seeing a form with the eye, he feels passion for pleasing forms and aversion for unpleasing forms. He dwells without an established sati of the physical body, with a restricted cetaso. He does not truly understand the liberation of cetaso and liberation by wisdom where those evil, unbeneficial dhammā cease without remainder. Engaged in favoring and opposing, whatever feeling he feels—ease, dukkha, or neither-dukkha-nor-ease—he delights in, welcomes, and remains attached to it. Delight arises. Whatever delight there is in feeling is upādāna. With upādāna as condition, being; with being as condition, birth; with birth as condition, aging and death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair arise. This is the origination of this entire mass of dukkha. (This applies to all six senses, up to the mano cognizing dhammā).

The Path to Liberation

“Here, the Tathāgata arises in the world, an arahant, perfectly self-awakened. He teaches the dhamma. A householder hears that dhamma, gains faith in the Tathāgata, and goes forth from home into homelessness, putting on the Kāsāya robes.

Endowed with the training and livelihood of those gone forth, he lives abandoning unbeneficial actions. He is content with robes to protect his physical body and almsfood to protect his belly. Endowed with this Noble aggregate of morality, he experiences an internally blameless ease.

Seeing a form with the eye, he does not grasp at its sign or secondary features. He practices sense restraint, protecting the mano-faculty, and experiencing an internally unsullied ease. He acts with clear comprehension in all activities.

“Resorting to a secluded dwelling, he abandons the five hindrances that defile the cetaso and weaken wisdom. Here, a monk, withdrawn from kāma and withdrawn from unbeneficial qualities, enters and remains in the first jhana, which is accompanied by vitakka and vicara, and possesses piti and sukha born of that withdrawal. Furthermore, with the stilling of vitakka and vicara, a monk enters and remains in the second jhana, which has internal confidence and collectedness of citta, is without vitakka and vicara, and has joy and sukha born of that samadhi. Furthermore, with dispassion for joy, he dwells with upekkhā, sati, clear comprehension, and experiences sukha with the body. He enters and remains in the third jhana, of which the Noble Ones declare: ‘He dwells with upekkhā, sati, and in sukha.’ Furthermore, with the abandoning of sukha and dukkha, and with the previous disappearance of mental pleasure and mental displeasure, he enters and remains in the fourth jhana, which is neither dukkha nor sukha, and has purity of sati and upekkhā.

“Seeing a form with the eye, he does not feel passion for pleasing forms nor aversion for unpleasing forms. He dwells with the established sati of the physical body, with an immeasurable cetaso. He truly understands the liberation of cetaso and liberation by wisdom where those evil, unbeneficial dhammā cease without remainder. Freed from favoring and opposing, whatever feeling he feels—ease, dukkha, or neither-dukkha-nor-ease—he does not delight in, welcome, or remain attached to it. Because he does not delight in it, whatever delight there is in feeling ceases. With the cessation of delight, upādāna ceases. With the cessation of upādāna, being ceases… and so the entire mass of dukkha ceases.

(This applies to all six senses, up to the mano cognizing dhammā).

“Hold in mind this brief exposition of liberation through the destruction of craving. Sāti, the fisherman’s son, however, is caught in the great net and tangle of craving.”

This is what the Buddha said. Uplifted, the bhikkhus delighted in the Buddha’s words.

© 2026 Just Dhamma
